agency_name: Commonwealth Scientific and Industrial Research Organisation (CSIRO)
executive_summary: CSIRO is Australia's national science agency (a corporate Commonwealth entity with significant own-source revenue) operating across six research areas plus national research infrastructure such as RV Investigator. The published 2025-26 plan landing page is extremely thin on detail, but its explicit framing around 'simplification and sustainability' confirms the dominant story external sources tell: CSIRO is mid-way through a financially-driven restructure targeting ~$100m in savings, with 800+ roles already cut (including ~25% of enterprise/support services) and further reductions flagged. This creates clear, defensible demand for operating-model and cost transformation, financial-sustainability advice, grants/compliance assurance (an ANAO focus), and workforce transition support. The constraint is equally clear: every external dollar must demonstrate hard value-for-money and withstand union, cross-bench and Senate Estimates scrutiny, and must not duplicate in-house capability.

strategic_priorities.title: Simplification and sustainability
strategic_priorities.description: CSIRO is explicitly reorienting toward increasing national impact 'through simplification and sustainability' — code for streamlining enterprise/support functions and putting the cost base on a sustainable footing while protecting research output.
strategic_priorities.source_quote: This Plan also reflects our focus on increasing CSIRO's impact for the nation through simplification and sustainability.

strategic_priorities.title: Delivering impact across six research areas
strategic_priorities.description: The strategy directs focus and investment across six research areas plus the national collaborative research infrastructure it manages on behalf of the nation, executed through strategic priorities and key initiatives that drive performance.
strategic_priorities.source_quote: Our strategy provides detail on our 6 research areas, as well as the infrastructure we manage for the nation and our underlying capabilities.

strategic_priorities.title: Partnering across the innovation ecosystem
strategic_priorities.description: CSIRO positions itself as a connector working with government, research organisations, industry and community to deliver impact — with co-investment partnerships (e.g. Ningaloo Outlook with Woodside) central to its funding model.
strategic_priorities.source_quote: We work with government, research organisations, industry and the community to deliver impact under the research areas.

reform_and_change_drivers: Financially-driven enterprise restructure targeting at least $100m in savings, including ~25% reduction in Enterprise Services (IT, finance, admin, comms, general management).
Operating costs (ICT and property) rising faster than inflation, and the expiry of a time-limited COVID-era budget measure, squeezing the cost base.
Decline in private-sector co-investment revenue (from $88.4m to $76.2m) intensifying value-for-money pressure on all spend.
Alignment expectations with the National Science and Research Priorities, framing decisions on research capability (notably climate/environment science).
Retrenchment of international/commercialisation footprint (e.g. closure of the US Delaware subsidiary).
challenges_and_risks: Sustaining research output while cutting support services by a quarter — risk of capability and continuity loss.
Politically and reputationally sensitive cuts to environment/climate-modelling capability under cross-bench and Senate scrutiny.
Rising employee and redundancy costs (redundancy spend ~$11m in 2023-24; employee costs up >$100m to $984m) against a thinning revenue base.
Grants administration and Commonwealth Grant Rules and Guidelines compliance under ANAO attention.
Historic property/asset-strategy weaknesses re-emerging under current cost pressure.
Heightened union sensitivity and intense external scrutiny of any new external consulting spend.

news_and_controversies.summary: CEO Dr Doug Hilton announced in 2024 that 375–500 Enterprise Services roles (IT, admin, finance, comms, general management) would be cut to save at least $100m; the Staff Association called it a 'dark day' and warned it could 'cripple research output.'
news_and_controversies.implication: Large support-services restructure signals demand for operating-model and shared-services redesign, but also intense scrutiny and budget constraint on any external spend.
news_and_controversies.source: ACS Information Age — https://ia.acs.org.au/article/2024/up-to-500-jobs-to-be-cut-at-csiro.html ; CSIRO Staff Association compilation — https://csirostaff.org.au/news/media/2024/07/01/recent-media-on-csiro-job-cuts/

news_and_controversies.summary: More than 800 jobs were cut after the first round in April 2024 with ~350 more flagged; the environment research unit lost 92 roles and was reduced from eight programs to five. Senator David Pocock criticised the reduction in 'sovereign climate modelling capacity.'
news_and_controversies.implication: Research-prioritisation and capability-exit decisions are politically charged — advisory work here must be defensible and aligned to national science priorities.

news_and_controversies.summary: CSIRO's 2023-24 accounts showed redundancy spend of ~$11m (up ~$4m), operating result down $3m to $14.7m, employee costs up >$100m to $984m, and private-sector revenue down from $88.4m to $76.2m (IP revenue $61m, a nine-year high).
news_and_controversies.implication: Falling co-investment revenue and a rising cost base reinforce a hard value-for-money lens — pitches must show ROI and avoid duplicating in-house capability.

news_and_controversies.summary: CSIRO's only US subsidiary, registered in Delaware as a commercialisation entity, was slated for closure in 2024 as part of the enterprise restructure.
news_and_controversies.implication: Indicates retrenchment of the international commercialisation footprint — opportunity lies in restructuring commercialisation models rather than expanding them.

anao_findings.summary: ANAO listed a potential performance audit on whether CSIRO's award and administration of funding is effective and consistent with the Commonwealth Grant Rules and Guidelines, examining programs including CSIRO Kick-Start, the SIEF Ross Metcalf Fellowship and SME Connect. Final tabling status was not confirmed.
anao_findings.implication: Grants compliance and CGRG alignment are a live ANAO focus at CSIRO — a credible assurance/advisory entry point if the audit proceeds or recommendations follow.

anao_findings.summary: An earlier (2020) ANAO performance audit found the design and implementation of CSIRO's 2012 Property Investment Strategy 'largely not effective,' with sites rising from 55 to 58 (2012–2019) even as building footprint fell ~10%. This predates the 3-year window and is background only.
anao_findings.implication: Property/asset strategy is a documented historical weakness, newly relevant given current ICT/property cost pressure.

parliamentary_matters.summary: At a Senate Economics Committee hearing on 7 November 2024, Dr Hilton testified the cuts were forced by ICT and property costs rising faster than inflation and the end of a COVID budget measure, requiring ~25% savings in enterprise services. On 25 November CSIRO confirmed RV Investigator would have full sea operations and no planned cuts to Data61.
parliamentary_matters.implication: Estimates is actively scrutinising the restructure, vessel funding and Data61 — engagements must be defensible under parliamentary scrutiny, and Data61/digital is a comparatively protected unit.

parliamentary_matters.summary: Independent Senator David Pocock publicly criticised the latest cuts, particularly to climate-modelling capacity, arguing they conflict with the National Science and Research Priorities.
parliamentary_matters.implication: Cross-bench attention raises reputational sensitivity around capability decisions; advisory positioning should emphasise alignment with stated national science priorities.

opportunity_areas.agency_need: Deliver ~25% / $100m savings in support functions (IT, finance, admin, comms) without crippling research output.
opportunity_areas.suggested_offering: A target operating model and shared-services design for Enterprise Services, defining future-state functions, service levels and governance — sized as a 8–16 week design piece with rollout support, paired with secondary Organisation, Workforce & People for the workforce transition.
opportunity_areas.rationale: The plan's 'simplification and sustainability' framing plus the confirmed $100m / 25% enterprise-services reduction (ACS, Senate testimony) make this a live, time-bound need; positioning must show it protects research capability.

opportunity_areas.agency_need: Arrest a rising cost base (employee costs +$100m, redundancy ~$11m) against falling private-sector co-investment revenue.
opportunity_areas.suggested_offering: A financial-sustainability review with activity-based costing of research and infrastructure functions to identify structural cost drivers and a value-for-money baseline that withstands Estimates scrutiny.
opportunity_areas.rationale: 2023-24 accounts show declining co-investment revenue and rising costs (Research Professional News); a credible costing/sustainability lens directly supports the simplification agenda and the value-for-money test the agency must meet.

opportunity_areas.agency_need: Manage 800+ role reductions and restructure with union sensitivity while retaining critical capability.
opportunity_areas.suggested_offering: Workforce planning and change-management support for the restructure — capability mapping, redeployment/retention planning and transition communications — delivered as an embedded or framework engagement.
opportunity_areas.rationale: Confirmed large-scale cuts and Staff Association concern about research impact (CSIRO Staff Association, Canberra Times) create a clear, sensitive workforce-transition need; right-sized and discreet given union/political attention.

opportunity_areas.agency_need: Ensure grant programs (Kick-Start, SME Connect, SIEF) align with the Commonwealth Grant Rules and Guidelines ahead of / following ANAO attention.
opportunity_areas.suggested_offering: An independent health check of grants allocation and administration against CGRG, with a remediation roadmap — a 4–10 week assurance review; secondary Procurement, Probity & Contracts for probity of award processes.
opportunity_areas.rationale: ANAO has scoped a performance audit on the effectiveness of CSIRO's grants allocation and administration (ANAO); a pre-emptive or responsive assurance review is a defensible, proportionate entry point.

opportunity_areas.agency_need: Reduce property/ICT costs rising faster than inflation, building on a historically weak property strategy.
opportunity_areas.suggested_offering: A property/asset financial review assessing footprint rationalisation options and whole-of-life cost, feeding the sustainability program.
opportunity_areas.rationale: Senate testimony cited property as a key cost driver, and a 2020 ANAO audit found the property strategy 'largely not effective' (ANAO) — a relevant, cost-led opportunity though the audit predates the 3-year window.

opportunity_areas.agency_need: Make and defend research capability decisions in line with the National Science and Research Priorities.
opportunity_areas.suggested_offering: An impact-prioritisation framework to support transparent, defensible investment and exit decisions across the six research areas.
opportunity_areas.rationale: Cross-bench criticism of climate-science cuts (Senator Pocock) shows decisions need a defensible evidence base aligned to national priorities; the plan's objectives are 'executed through strategic priorities and key initiatives,' implying a prioritisation mechanism. Fit is inferred rather than named.

recommended_actions.action: Request a meeting with the Chief Operating Officer / Enterprise Services leadership to discuss operating-model and shared-services design that protects research capability while delivering the $100m savings.
recommended_actions.rationale: This is the agency's defining live program; the COO/Enterprise Services function owns the savings target and is actively reshaping support functions.

recommended_actions.action: Prepare a concise financial-sustainability and activity-based-costing capability statement tailored to a research agency with falling co-investment revenue, and route it to the CFO.
recommended_actions.rationale: The cost/revenue squeeze is documented and ongoing; a costing-led, value-for-money pitch matches both the simplification agenda and the scrutiny environment.

recommended_actions.action: Monitor the ANAO work program for the CSIRO grants-administration audit and prepare a grants/CGRG assurance offer to the head of grants/SME Connect and the Audit Committee.
recommended_actions.rationale: An open or imminent ANAO audit is a strong, time-bound assurance demand signal; being ready when recommendations land is decisive.

recommended_actions.action: Develop a discreet workforce-transition / change-management proposition mindful of union and parliamentary sensitivity, for the Chief People Officer.
recommended_actions.rationale: Large-scale restructure is underway; sensitively framed transition support addresses a real need without inflaming reputational risk.

recommended_actions.action: Watch Senate Economics Committee Estimates and Hansard for further commitments on the restructure, vessel funding and Data61, and verify the ANAO audit status directly.
recommended_actions.rationale: The restructure is under active parliamentary scrutiny; emerging commitments will shape procurement timing and the politics of external spend.

engagement_targets: Chief Operating Officer / Enterprise Services leadership (owns the $100m simplification program)
Chief Financial Officer (cost base, co-investment revenue, financial sustainability)
Chief People Officer / HR leadership (workforce transition and restructure)
Head of grants programs (CSIRO Kick-Start, SME Connect, SIEF) and the Audit and Risk Committee
Data61 / Digital leadership (a comparatively protected, digitally-focused unit)
Chief Risk Officer / internal audit function (assurance and CGRG compliance)
talking_points: Your 'simplification and sustainability' agenda is exactly where independent operating-model and costing support adds value — we can help deliver the $100m without eroding research output.
Any external spend will be scrutinised at Estimates and by the Staff Association; we design engagements to be defensible on value-for-money and to complement, not duplicate, your in-house teams.
With co-investment revenue falling and costs rising, an activity-based costing baseline gives leadership a transparent foundation for the savings program.
The ANAO's interest in grants administration is an opportunity to get ahead of findings with a focused CGRG health check.
Research-prioritisation decisions are under cross-bench scrutiny — a defensible impact framework aligned to the National Science and Research Priorities protects both capability and reputation.
Property and ICT cost drivers flagged in Estimates are addressable through a focused asset-strategy and footprint review.
caveats: The corporate plan page supplied is almost entirely boilerplate and navigation — it contains no detailed key activities, risk register, capability section or performance measures, so strategic priorities are inferred from the single 'simplification and sustainability' statement plus the research-area framing. The substantive BD signals come from external research (restructure, savings, ANAO grants audit, Estimates), some of which carries unconfirmed status (the ANAO audit tabling status and the parliamentary items are corroborated via media rather than verified against aph.gov.au/anao.gov.au directly). Opportunities are therefore weighted toward documented financial/restructure pressures rather than the plan text itself. The intense scrutiny and budget constraint mean appetite for external consulting may be limited and politically sensitive — proposals must be lean, value-for-money and non-duplicative.
